anomaly_probe.anomaly_candidate: A reflective person in grief, moral repair, trauma integration, or non-instrumental practice who is harmed by stopping on schedule because the timer becomes avoidance, emotional bypassing, or a new compulsive ritual.
anomaly_probe.confidence_effect: Lowers confidence in broad teaching status but preserves the model as a narrow practice-fit rule for screened over-reflectors.
anomaly_probe.why_it_breaks_or_strains_model: The model depends on the problem being over-reflection in search of certainty. In grief, confession, repair, trauma integration, or practice-realization, the needed process may be spacious, relational, recurring, or embodied rather than closed for the day by a timer. A timer may also become the reassurance ritual that ERP would target.

claim_units: Some reflective practices lack a reliable natural terminus.
For compulsive over-reflectors, felt resolution can be an unreliable and self-perpetuating completion criterion.
A precommitted external limit, time or one concrete act, should close reflection for the day.
Continuing past the chosen limit is often avoidance, reassurance seeking, or perfectionism disguised as diligence.
The rule is contraindicated for under-reflection, acute grief, trauma processing, moral repair, clinical obsessive doubt without support, and non-instrumental practice-realization traditions.

cross_domain_prediction.prediction: On low-risk tasks, timeboxed review with a written stop rule will reduce churn, duplicate comments, and reopened decisions without reducing defect detection compared with review-until-satisfied.
cross_domain_prediction.structural_translation: A review or refactor can lack a natural internal feeling of done. For perfectionistic teams or individuals, completion should be defined by a precommitted timebox, acceptance criterion, or one bounded action, not by the feeling that the design is finally clean.
cross_domain_prediction.would_count_against_it: Defects increase, useful design concerns are suppressed, or ordinary issue scoping and Definition of Done explain all observed benefits.

exact_claim: Some reflective work has no natural internal end, especially for people prone to compulsive self-examination, perfectionism, or achievement-contingent self-worth. For those cases, completion should be defined before starting by an external limit, such as a fixed time or one bounded action, and the question should close for the day even if it does not feel resolved. A method has surrendered its hold when the practitioner can stop on schedule, not when reflection finally feels complete.

falsifiable_predictions.expected_observation: A fixed-time or one-act close reduces same-day reopening, rumination, self-worth distress, and redundant journaling compared with reflect-until-resolved instructions.
falsifiable_predictions.if_model_is_right: For compulsive over-reflectors, external limits outperform felt-resolution stopping.
falsifiable_predictions.test_surface: Randomized two-week pilot with screened over-reflectors, daily logs, rumination scales, distress ratings, and action-completion measures.
falsifiable_predictions.would_weaken_or_falsify: The external limit performs no better, increases anxiety, becomes a ritual, or reduces responsible action.

falsifiable_predictions.expected_observation: High-rumination and high-perfectionism participants benefit more than under-reflectors, grievers, and avoidant participants.
falsifiable_predictions.if_model_is_right: The benefit is moderated by failure mode.
falsifiable_predictions.test_surface: Moderator analysis comparing over-reflection, avoidance, grief, and perfectionism profiles.
falsifiable_predictions.would_weaken_or_falsify: The timer helps equally across groups, or mainly helps by ordinary scheduling rather than by reducing compulsive resolution seeking.

falsifiable_predictions.expected_observation: People who endlessly edit adequate documents, plans, or designs complete more work with bounded stopping rules, without lower blind quality ratings.
falsifiable_predictions.if_model_is_right: The same structure travels to non-introspective over-refinement.
falsifiable_predictions.test_surface: Writing, planning, or design experiment with blind quality ratings and revision-count metrics.
falsifiable_predictions.would_weaken_or_falsify: Quality drops sharply, avoidance rises, or ordinary deadlines explain all benefits.

falsifiable_predictions.expected_observation: Teachers in non-instrumental traditions report that a timed close may be useful for session discipline but does not mark existential completion or method surrender.
falsifiable_predictions.if_model_is_right: Practice-realization and other non-instrumental contexts are true scope exceptions.
falsifiable_predictions.test_surface: Practitioner interviews with Soto Zen, Dzogchen, liturgical, and devotional teachers.
falsifiable_predictions.would_weaken_or_falsify: Such practitioners endorse the timer as a valid completion criterion for the practice's core aim.

near_neighbors.difference: The candidate applies the stop-rule insight to spiritual self-review and method-release instructions, and recommends a clock or bounded act for non-clinical over-reflectors.
near_neighbors.novelty_impact: Major reduction. The core mechanism is known, so the idea is mostly a renamed application with a spiritual frame.
near_neighbors.overlap: Extremely close. This literature already treats perseveration as governed by stop rules and mood, and contrasts continuing until one feels satisfied with rule-based stopping conditions.
near_neighbors.source: Mood-as-input and stop-rule literature on worry, rumination, and checking, including MacDonald and Davey on stop rules and perseverative checking, and Watkins and Mason on mood-as-input and rumination.

near_neighbors.difference: The source studies compulsive washing, while the candidate addresses ordinary reflective practice and spiritual self-audit.
near_neighbors.novelty_impact: Major reduction for the felt-completion claim.
near_neighbors.overlap: Very close on the exact danger of using a felt rightness signal as the criterion for stopping.
near_neighbors.source: Wahl et al., I wash until it feels right: the phenomenology of stopping criteria in obsessive-compulsive washing, Journal of Anxiety Disorders 2008, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18221894/.

near_neighbors.difference: The candidate generalizes the logic to ordinary reflection and spiritual self-review, with a time or bounded-action close rather than formal exposure protocol.
near_neighbors.novelty_impact: Major reduction and a strong safety constraint.
near_neighbors.overlap: Very close on refusing the internal demand for certainty, stopping reassurance or checking behaviors, and tolerating unresolved doubt.
near_neighbors.source: ERP and CBT literature on OCD, compulsive checking, reassurance seeking, and intolerance of uncertainty, including International OCD Foundation materials on ERP, https://iocdf.org/about-ocd/treatment/erp/.

near_neighbors.difference: The candidate uses contemplative self-canceling method as the frame and proposes a simple external close for non-clinical use.
near_neighbors.novelty_impact: Strong reduction for the psychological mechanism and practice design.
near_neighbors.overlap: Close on the idea that perseverative self-focused analysis maintains distress, and that changing the process of thinking matters more than finding the perfect answer.
near_neighbors.source: Rumination-focused CBT and metacognitive therapy for rumination and worry, for example Watkins' rumination-focused CBT work and Wells' metacognitive therapy literature.

near_neighbors.difference: The candidate applies precommitment to spiritual or reflective completion rather than general goal pursuit.
near_neighbors.novelty_impact: Moderate to strong reduction for the behavioral mechanism.
near_neighbors.overlap: Close on precommitted action rules that replace deliberation in moments where ongoing evaluation would derail behavior.
near_neighbors.source: Gollwitzer implementation intentions and precommitment research, for example implementation-intention reviews in goal pursuit literature.

near_neighbors.difference: Most productivity methods do not address contemplative method-release, compulsive certainty, or self-worth distress.
near_neighbors.novelty_impact: Strong reduction for the practical stopping rule.
near_neighbors.overlap: Close on defining completion externally by time, scope, or agreed done criteria rather than by an unstable sense of perfect completion.
near_neighbors.source: Productivity methods: timeboxing, Pomodoro, WIP limits, and Definition of Done, with timeboxing commonly defined as assigning a fixed period to an activity.

near_neighbors.difference: This candidate is cleaner at the individual practice level: stop by clock or bounded act, not by felt completion.
near_neighbors.novelty_impact: Major internal reduction. Treat as a focused practice variant, not a discovery.
near_neighbors.overlap: Close internal prior art. These already argue for stopping, gating, and blocking repeated refinement when it adds no new source, anomaly, prediction, or test.
near_neighbors.source: Prior Lumenary records: More Is Not The Same As Progress, Restating Is Not Realizing, The Silence Has A Stopping Rule, Not Every Check Should Be Yours, and The Answer You Keep Finding Is Already Settled.

near_neighbors.difference: The candidate identifies a missing terminus and imports external stopping rules for over-reflectors.
near_neighbors.novelty_impact: Moderate reduction. The old source names release, not the behavioral anti-compulsion protocol.
near_neighbors.overlap: Close on the self-canceling or disposable method structure.
near_neighbors.source: Wittgenstein Tractatus 6.54 and Buddhist raft-simile traditions.

near_neighbors.difference: The candidate explicitly scopes out practice-realization but still risks importing instrumental timeboxing into non-instrumental practice.
near_neighbors.novelty_impact: Limits generality and lowers publishability as doctrine.
near_neighbors.overlap: Not a support source, but a structural anomaly: practice may not be an instrument that closes when a clock expires or an action is completed.
near_neighbors.source: Dogen practice-realization, non-gaining zazen, and other non-instrumental practice traditions.

next_loop_instructions: Do not publish as a standalone discovery. Cross-index with More Is Not The Same As Progress, Restating Is Not Realizing, The Silence Has A Stopping Rule, Not Every Check Should Be Yours, and The Answer You Keep Finding Is Already Settled.
Rewrite as a bounded practice-fit protocol for over-reflection, not as a general teaching about method surrender.
Add a triage gate before the practice: over-reflection versus avoidance, grief, moral repair, trauma, OCD or scrupulosity, and non-instrumental practice.

Compare directly against mood-as-input stop-rule training, ERP response prevention, rumination-focused CBT, metacognitive therapy, timeboxing, WIP limits, implementation intentions, and ordinary journaling limits.
Pilot only with screened non-clinical over-reflectors. Track reopening frequency, anxiety, ritualization, avoidance, shame, action completion, and whether the closing act becomes compulsive.
novelty_adjustment: Lower novelty from 0.55 to 0.24. The narrow contribution is a spiritual framing of established stop-rule, ERP, rumination, and timeboxing principles. The strongest close neighbor is mood-as-input stop-rule research, which already attacks felt completion as a dangerous stopping criterion. Human-condition fit is strong for perfectionists, chronic self-improvers, over-journalers, and achievement-contingent over-reflectors. It is not for acute grief, trauma processing, moral repair, clinical OCD or scrupulosity without trained care, under-reflection, coercive workload, or non-instrumental practice where timed stopping would distort the aim.

practitioner_test.answers_that_reduce_originality: Clinicians and teachers say this is standard stop-rule work, response prevention, postponement, timeboxing, or journaling hygiene, and that the spiritual-method framing adds no new screening or prediction.
practitioner_test.answers_that_support_contribution: Practitioners find that the model reliably identifies a neglected failure mode: people understand release intellectually but keep applying release-instructions compulsively, and a bounded external close helps where insight-based advice fails.
questions: Is this more than mood-as-input stop-rule training, ERP response prevention, uncertainty tolerance, rumination-focused CBT, or ordinary timeboxing?
How would you distinguish an over-reflector helped by a timer from an avoidant person using the timer to evade honesty?
When does stopping on time become a new compulsion or reassurance ritual?
In your practice context, does a timed close mark completion, or only a practical boundary around a session?
What concrete failure cases would make you forbid this practice or require clinician involvement?
relevant_practitioners: ERP clinicians working with OCD, scrupulosity, reassurance seeking, and checking
CBT or metacognitive therapists treating rumination and worry
Spiritual directors familiar with scrupulosity and discernment
Meditation teachers familiar with non-striving and adverse practice loops
Soto Zen or other practice-realization teachers
Writing teachers and editors working with perfectionistic over-revision
Productivity coaches using timeboxing or WIP limits

primary_pair: Wittgenstein, Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus 6.54, ladder image, https://www.gutenberg.org/files/5740/5740-h/5740-h.htm; MN 22 Alagaddupama Sutta raft simile, https://www.dhammatalks.org/suttas/MN/MN22.html
Heart Sutra, https://www.fgsitc.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Heart-Sutra-2016.pdf; Dogen practice-realization sources such as Bendowa or Genjokoan.
primary_text_close_read.risk_of_overread: High in clinical, grief, trauma, and moral-repair contexts. A timer can become reassurance, ritual, avoidance, or self-punishment. The model can mistake legitimate repeated practice, mourning, confession, discernment, and non-instrumental sitting for compulsive reopening. It should be framed as a triage rule for a specific failure mode, not as a general spiritual law.
primary_text_close_read.secondary_literature_gap: The strongest near-neighbors are not only spiritual texts but clinical and cognitive stop-rule literatures. The audit needs full reading in mood-as-input studies, ERP manuals, intolerance-of-uncertainty protocols, rumination-focused CBT, metacognitive therapy, implementation intentions, and timeboxing research. It also needs practitioner checks from Zen and other non-instrumental practice contexts.
primary_text_close_read.structural_observation: Tractatus 6.54 and MN 22 both picture a method that should not be retained as an object after it has served its function. Neither source gives a modern behavioral stopping rule for a person whose compulsion is to keep checking whether they have really let go. The Heart Sutra complicates discard language because it negates attainment while preserving reliance. Dogen complicates it more sharply because practice may not be an instrument whose completion is externally marked. The candidate's distinct structural claim is not self-canceling method, which is old, but the external terminus for people whose inner completion signal is unreliable.

title: Stop When Time Is Up, Not When It Feels Done
unlike_statement: Unlike mood-as-input stop-rule research, obsessive-compulsive stopping-criteria studies, ERP, rumination therapies, timeboxing, implementation intentions, WIP limits, and prior Lumenary stopping-rule records, this idea says that self-canceling spiritual instructions fail for over-reflectors unless completion is moved outside felt resolution into a precommitted clock or bounded act. That combination is usable, but the mechanism is mostly already known.
